record.field: Peppermint is an open-source, self-hosted helpdesk and ticket management platform, a lightweight alternative to Zendesk, Freshdesk and Jira Service Management.

Peppermint is an open-source, self-hosted helpdesk and ticket management platform, a lightweight alternative to Zendesk, Freshdesk and Jira Service Management.

It lets internal teams and customer-facing support desks capture, track and resolve requests through a clean web interface, turning emails into tickets automatically and keeping every customer interaction in one place. Built on a TypeScript/React frontend with a Node.js backend and PostgreSQL, it runs comfortably on low-end hardware while remaining scalable for larger deployments. It is aimed at small businesses, homelabs and teams that want structured support workflows without the complexity of full ITSM suites.

Key features
  • Email-to-ticket conversion over SMTP/IMAP for streamlined intake
  • Ticket creation with a Markdown editor and file attachments
  • OIDC single sign-on integrating with providers like Keycloak and Authentik
  • Webhooks and integrations to connect external tools and automate workflows
  • Built-in analytics for ticket performance and team productivity
  • Client history log plus a Markdown notebook with task lists; responsive UI from mobile to 4K
Best for

Small businesses, makerspaces, homelab enthusiasts and support teams that need a simple, privacy-friendly, self-hosted service desk for internal staff and customer requests, without the overhead of heavyweight ITSM platforms.

services: peppermint-postgres: image: postgres:latest restart: always volumes: - ./peppermint_postgres_data:/var/lib/postgresql/data environment: POSTGRES_USER: peppermint POSTGRES_PASSWORD: {{ postgres_password }} POSTGRES_DB: peppermint healthcheck: test: - CMD-SHELL - pg_isready -U peppermint interval: 10s timeout: 5s retries: 5 container_name: '{{ INSTANCE_CODE }}_peppermint_postgres' networks: - internal peppermint-app: image: pepperlabs/peppermint:latest restart: always depends_on: peppermint-postgres: condition: service_healthy environment: DB_USERNAME: peppermint DB_PASSWORD: {{ postgres_password }} DB_HOST: peppermint-postgres SECRET: {{ secret }} container_name: '{{ INSTANCE_CODE }}' ports: - '{{ PORT_HTTP }}:3000' networks: - internal - agz-proxy-network networks: internal: driver: bridge agz-proxy-network: external: true

#!/bin/bash set -euo pipefail cd "$SERVICE_PATH" # Dump consistente de PostgreSQL (servicio en marcha) → $BACKUP_DATA_DIR docker compose exec -T peppermint-postgres sh -c 'pg_dump -U "$POSTGRES_USER" -d "$POSTGRES_DB" --no-owner -Fc' \ > "$BACKUP_DATA_DIR/db.dump" echo "pg_dump OK ($(wc -c < "$BACKUP_DATA_DIR/db.dump") bytes)"

#!/bin/bash set -euo pipefail cd "$SERVICE_PATH" # Levanta solo la DB, espera readiness y restaura docker compose up -d peppermint-postgres for i in $(seq 1 30); do docker compose exec -T peppermint-postgres sh -c 'pg_isready -U "$POSTGRES_USER"' && break sleep 2 done docker compose exec -T peppermint-postgres sh -c 'pg_restore -U "$POSTGRES_USER" -d "$POSTGRES_DB" --clean --if-exists --no-owner' \ < "$RESTORE_DATA_DIR/db.dump" # Verificación: la DB no quedó vacía (sh -c con dobles comillas; $VAR del contenedor con \$) N=$(docker compose exec -T peppermint-postgres sh -c "psql -U \$POSTGRES_USER -d \$POSTGRES_DB -tAc \"SELECT count(*) FROM information_schema.tables WHERE table_schema='public'\"") echo "tablas restauradas: $N" test "$N" -gt 0
